story:
The building groans as Jules climbs the failing fire escape, heat on her face, comm crackling in her ear. Cam’s voice is steady: “Left at the next window. The stairwell collapsed — you’ll need to rappel.” On the street, cameras and drones drink her in. The city sees a hero. No one sees the man three blocks away, alone with maps, monitors, and worry, guiding her step by step.

He tracks structural stress and crowd flow, fingers flying. “Civilians in 3B,” he says. Smoke thickens. A child cries. Jules breaks the lock, gets the family moving, shields them through a window to safety. A teenage girl asks, “Are you scared?” Jules tells the truth: “Every time.”

When the sirens fade and the lenses point elsewhere, Jules is a shadow on a different rooftop across the block, one the fire never touched. She pushes her mask up, shoulder bleeding, and stares at a city that will never know the whole story. Cam arrives with coffee. They sit in the quiet gold of sunrise. He’s the only one who knows the parts the viral clips don’t show — the panic attacks, the nightmares, the flinch when a voice snaps.

“You okay?” he asks. “Define okay,” she says.

She wonders if they’re helping or just moving the damage around. Yesterday she saved a bus full of kids and left three buildings scarred in the process. Today’s fire started because people hunting for her showed up at her place. Cam doesn’t argue. He lets the doubt breathe and sits in it with her.

“Can you keep a secret?” she asks. He nods. “I hate being a hero.” The words land hard. She didn’t train for this. She didn’t ask. One day the world changed, and with it, her. Now there are expectations — brave, noble, selfless — while she walks around terrified, missing the safety of being no one.

Cam thinks. Then: “Being a hero isn’t about wanting it. It’s about showing up anyway. Even when you’re scared. Especially when you’re scared.” She calls it comic-book talk, but she knows it’s true. She pulls down the mask, hides the tears, and thanks him for the coffee. At the door she stops him. Don’t tell anyone. She doesn’t want to shake people’s faith. “Spoken like a true hero,” he says, and promises to keep her secret.

He doesn’t go home. He lingers in the stairwell with an empty cup and a full heart. Pride, envy, guilt. They were kids together once, invisible, tucked in a library corner, dreaming of mattering. Then her powers arrived. The city claimed her face and name. He never resented her — not really — but sometimes it feels like watching someone else live the story they imagined together.

He wants to be brave. To be seen. To matter. He has no powers. Just a good heart and a front-row seat to someone else’s legend. And now he knows she hates the legend. He swallows the bitterness. It isn’t her fault. She didn’t choose any of it, yet she shows up anyway, even when it costs her. Maybe that’s the difference. He had wanted the glory. She carries the weight.

Cam straightens, resolves, and descends. He’ll keep her secret, not just because she asked, but because he understands now. Being a hero isn’t being seen. It’s showing up. And in his quiet way, he will keep showing up too.
